title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read an article about scientific research and curiosity. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Question Behind the Question
content.subtitle: An article about curiosity as the driving force of science.
content.text:
In 1928, Alexander Fleming noticed that a mould had contaminated one of his bacterial cultures and killed the bacteria around it. A less curious scientist might have thrown out the ruined plate and started again. Fleming looked more carefully. [31] That moment of redirected attention — the choice to investigate rather than discard — led, within two decades, to the mass production of penicillin and the saving of millions of lives. [/31] Science, at its most consequential, often begins not with a hypothesis but with a question provoked by something unexpected.

Curiosity is a slippery concept to define scientifically, but researchers who study it describe it as a motivational state triggered by an information gap — the uncomfortable awareness that there is something you do not know but could. [32] Psychologists George Loewenstein and Celeste Kidd describe curiosity as inherently uncomfortable: it is not the pleasure of knowing, but the itch of not-knowing that drives the curious person forward. [/32] The desire to close the gap, they argue, is what makes scientific inquiry feel less like work and more like compulsion.

This framing has implications for how we educate children. Traditional school science tends to present knowledge as a body of established facts to be transferred from teacher to student — a process that satisfies the teacher but may do little to awaken curiosity in the learner. [33] Several educators argue that the more effective approach is to present science as a set of open questions, to let students feel the itch before offering the scratch. [/33] Experiments in Finnish and Canadian schools that adopted this approach reported higher engagement and significantly better long-term retention of scientific concepts.

The relationship between curiosity and risk is equally important. Genuinely curious scientists tend to be comfortable with uncertainty in ways their less curious colleagues are not. [34] Where others see a failed experiment, the curious researcher sees a result — unexpected, perhaps, but informative. [/34] This psychological tolerance for ambiguity is not merely a personality trait; it can be cultivated, and institutions that reward only positive results actively select against it, creating a research culture that is more cautious and less innovative than it needs to be.

Funding bodies have begun to recognise this. Several major research councils have introduced 'blue-sky' funding streams specifically designed to support curiosity-driven research with no predetermined application. Critics argue that public money should be directed towards problems with clear social benefit. [35] Proponents counter that the history of science is littered with discoveries made in pursuit of pure curiosity that later transformed medicine, engineering, and technology in ways no one anticipated at the time. [/35] Penicillin is one example; so are X-rays, the internet, and GPS.

None of which changes the fact that curiosity can be an uncomfortable and professionally risky quality in an academic environment designed around publication targets and grant cycles. [36] The scientists who reshape fields are often those who are willing to seem foolish — to ask the obvious question, to pursue the unpromising lead, to stay longer than is sensible with a result that doesn't fit. [/36] Curiosity, in other words, requires a kind of courage that no curriculum explicitly teaches.

questions.question: The example of Alexander Fleming is used to illustrate that
options: A) accidents in the laboratory are more common than people realise.
B) important scientific discoveries can result from paying attention to the unexpected.
C) penicillin was discovered later than it should have been.
D) scientists in the 1920s were more open-minded than those today.
questions.explanation: Fleming's 'redirected attention — the choice to investigate rather than discard' led to penicillin, showing that attending to unexpected events drives major discoveries.

questions.question: According to Loewenstein and Kidd, curiosity is best described as
options: A) the pleasure experienced when a problem is finally solved.
B) an unpleasant feeling caused by being aware of a gap in one's knowledge.
C) a natural instinct that weakens as people get older.
D) a learned behaviour that develops through formal education.
questions.explanation: They describe it as 'the itch of not-knowing' and as 'inherently uncomfortable' — an unpleasant awareness of a knowledge gap.

questions.question: The educational approach described as more effective involves
options: A) giving students access to the most up-to-date scientific information.
B) having students conduct more experiments and fewer written exercises.
C) exposing students to open questions before providing answers.
D) allowing students to choose the scientific topics they study.
questions.explanation: The approach is described as 'to present science as a set of open questions, to let students feel the itch before offering the scratch' — experiencing the question before the answer.

questions.question: The writer suggests that curious researchers differ from less curious ones because they
options: A) are more likely to repeat failed experiments until they succeed.
B) treat unexpected results as useful information rather than failures.
C) are less concerned about the practical applications of their work.
D) have a greater ability to identify experimental errors.
questions.explanation: The text states that 'Where others see a failed experiment, the curious researcher sees a result — unexpected, perhaps, but informative', meaning they find value in unexpected outcomes.

questions.question: Those who support 'blue-sky' funding argue that
options: A) pure research is more reliable than applied research.
B) research councils have a duty to fund all types of scientific inquiry.
C) discoveries driven by curiosity have historically produced major practical benefits.
D) funding bodies are currently too focused on short-term results.
questions.explanation: Proponents point to 'discoveries made in pursuit of pure curiosity that later transformed medicine, engineering, and technology', citing examples like penicillin and GPS.

questions.question: In the final paragraph, the writer implies that curiosity in academic environments requires
options: A) significant changes to how universities are funded.
B) bravery, as it means risking one's professional reputation.
C) a natural talent that cannot be learned or developed.
D) the support of colleagues who share the same values.
questions.explanation: The writer says curious scientists must be 'willing to seem foolish' and describes this as a 'kind of courage', implying it requires bravery to pursue curiosity professionally.
